QF.id: XIII Trading, Clearing, Settlement and Risk Management_57

question.text: In currency derivatives trading, when a trading member's collateral utilization reaches 90% of the available limit, what restriction is triggered?

answer.text: Risk reduction mode — only square-off orders are permitted
Trading is completely suspended for the member
New positions are allowed but with double margin
An alert is sent but no trading restriction is imposed

explanation.text: **The Logic:** At 90% collateral utilization, the exchange places the trading member in risk reduction mode where only square-off (closing) orders are accepted. This prevents the member from increasing exposure when already near limits. At 85%, auto square-off of positions is triggered. **The Trap:** Complete suspension is too extreme — the member can still close positions. Double margin is not the mechanism used. A mere alert without restriction would fail to manage risk. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series I (Currency Derivatives) specifies the 90% and 85% thresholds. Equity derivatives (Series VIII) have similar risk management frameworks but the specific trigger levels may differ. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Risk reduction mode is a safety net — distributors should ensure clients maintain comfortable margin buffers well below 90% to avoid forced square-offs at unfavorable prices.
